comments.text: In [EGIP96] (also mentioned in #419), the authors prove $\liminf_{n \rightarrow \infty} F(c \log n, n) \le 2$ for any constant $c > 0$, using ideas used in bounds on large prime gaps (which means that this result can perhaps be slightly improved by the resolution of #4, although I haven't checked), while $\liminf_{n \rightarrow \infty} F(n^{4/9}, n) \ge 2$. In fact, their results/proofs give $\liminf_{n \rightarrow \infty} F(c \log n, n) = 1$ and $\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} F(n^{4/9}, n) = \infty$ respectively. This exponent $4/9$ can certainly be improved a little, but I'm not sure by how much. So as far as I can tell, $f(n) = n^{4/9}$ is the slowest growing function for which it is known that $F(f, n)$ goes to infinity with $n$. And for those interested, let me quickly say a few words on the idea behind the proof of this result. By Lemma $1$ in their paper, the ratio $\frac{\tau(n!)}{\tau((n-1)!)}$ is well-approximated by $1 + \frac{S(n)}{n}$, where $S(n)$ is the sum of the prime factors of $n$ (counted with multiplicity). Hence, to determine $F(f, n)$ it is more or less sufficient to determine the value of $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{f(n)} S(n+i)$. In particular, if you have sufficiently many $n+i \in (n, n + f(n))$ which are divisible by large primes, then those $S(n+i)$ will also be large, and thereby so will $F(f, n)$ be. All in all, proving bounds on the number of integers in short intervals that have a large prime factor, captures the essential difficulty of this problem. Note by the way that $F(1, n) \le 2$ with equality if, and only if, $n+1$ is prime, so that $\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} F(g(n) \log^2 n, n) = \infty$ follows from Cramér's conjecture for any function $g(n)$ that goes to infinity with $n$. Unconditionally, for such functions $g(n)$ we have $\limsup_{n \rightarrow \infty} F(g(n), n) = \infty$, by results on bounded prime gaps. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
